title: Convert to emit 'loose methods' with explicit 'self' pointers
body:
This is a bigish change. It implements #2988  (which itself is on the way to #2958 and #2140). There are some minor differences from what was discussed in #2988 but the result is hopefully more practicable.

It is still WIP for the reasons raised below, on which I would like some advice, but it is mostly there. Regressions, ext tests pass and https://github.com/lowRISC/opentitan builds.

I tried to keep the commits self consistent and tests shold pass at all of them, in case this helps review.

I will try to summarize the effective change in the output code at the end of this patch queue.

Internally, there are 2 ways to reference variables/cells (as in V3Descope). Relative addressing via `this`, or absolute addressing via `vlSyms`. This means any internal function may require access to these 2 values.

In this PR, all internal functions that are under an `AstNodeModule` (except under `AstClass`) have been changed to receive an explicit `self` pointer as a single argument, and then converted to what I call 'loose methods'. Loose methods are semantically methods in the sense that they associate with a particular class, but they are declared as global functions, so they don't need to be declared in the header (which removes the dependency causing recompilatoin). This means the standard signature changed from:
```
<ModuleClass>::<funcname>(Syms* vlSyms);
```
to:
```
<ModuleClass>__<funcname>(<ModuleClass>* self);
```

In all modules bar AstClass, `vlSyms` is available via `self->vlSyms`. There are hints towards aliasing issues in V3Descope, but these seem to relate to vintage compliers, the workaround for which (--no-relative-cfuncs) is already deprecated. At a later date we should change things to use relative addressing wherever possible for simplicity. The issue seems to be the potential aliasing between `self` and `vlSyms->SOMETHING_WIHT_TYPE_OF_SELF`. I think if this ever comes out again, we should just teach Verilator to emmit better code that work with mixed references by the judiscious use or `restrict`, unless you think we need to worry about vintage compilers. Comments or concerns on this are welcome.

There is no change to methdos under an `AstClass`, they are emitted as methods before, and `vlSyms` is passed as a parameter as before.

To keep Verilator internals simple, relative references are always emitted as an acces via explicit poitner access by emitting `this->...`, and aboslute references are emitted as `vlSyms->...`. For normal methods, this just works because `this` is available, and `vlSyms` either comes from a function argument (for `AstClass` members) or a class member (other `AstNodeModule` members). For loose methods, we emit a standard prologue and epliloge of the form:
```
#define this self
#define vlSyms self->vlSyms
  <body>
#undef this
#undef vlSyms
```

Once we introduced loose methods, we now no longer need to declare them in the module header, so we just declare them lazily as needed.

This all seems to work. I run some basic benchmarking on EH1, and it looks ok:
```
Platform:
- Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-5930K
- Turbo boost disabled
- CPU frequency pinned to 3.50GHz
- Everything run single threaded on core 0
- Tracing to ramdisk
- System otherwise idle
- Ubuntu 20.04.2 LTS
- Compilers installed via apt

################################################################################
# EH1 - no tracing

make -f $RV_ROOT/tools/Makefile \
  VERILATOR=$VERILATOR \
  CONF_PARAMS="-iccm_enable=1 -fpga_optimize" \
  target=high_perf \
  TEST=cmark_iccm \
  verilator-build program.hex

numactl -C 0 perf stat -r 10 ./obj_dir/Vtb_top

+--------------+----------------+----------------+
| compiler     | loose methods  | 1f331bd9       |
+==============+================+================+
| gcc    9.3.0 | 18.30 +- 0.004 | 18.33 +- 0.004 |
| clang 10.0.0 | 19.44 +- 0.006 | 19.49 +- 0.005 |

####################
# EH1 - tracing

NOTE: testbench/test_tb_top.cpp modified to write sim.vcd to ramdisk

make -f $RV_ROOT/tools/Makefile \
  VERILATOR=$VERILATOR \
  CONF_PARAMS="-iccm_enable=1 -fpga_optimize" \
  target=high_perf \
  TEST=cmark_iccm \
  debug=1 \
  verilator-build program.hex

numactl -C 0 perf stat --pre "rm -f /ramdisk/sim.vcd" -r 10 ./obj_dir/Vtb_top +dumpon

+--------------+----------------+----------------+
| compiler     | loose methods  | 1f331bd9       |
+==============+================+================+
| gcc    9.3.0 | 55.81 +- 0.004 | 55.75 +- 0.008 |
| clang 10.0.0 | 42.62 +- 0.005 | 42.69 +- 0.014 |
```

The reason the PR is still WIP:
- I actually need to test with the hashing base code splitting I have been working on that this actually works, but it addresses the issues I saw previously. I will do this next.
- You might want to run some more performance benchmarks. Sadly I don't have access to large designs to do much more testing. I requested some suggestions from the opentitan team for using that project as a benchmark (looking at you @rswarbrick) as it seems to be of sufficient size.
- In all of this, there is a need to figure out which `AstNodeModule` a particular `AstCFunc` is under (for cross module references), I have been using the following gross hack to do this, which is not ideal:
https://github.com/gezalore/verilator/blob/a31b3fbed6ae904e3b2b57ebbc5899a0bb39af75/src/V3EmitC.cpp#L391-L393
Note in later patches there will be a need finding the module of any `AstVar` as well (again for cross module references to static constants for example), in which case this hack is not only gross but is a performance problem due to `backp` going backwards on a long list before moving up the tree at the head of the list. Having this mechanism also allows us to get rid of the `classPrefix` member from `AstVarRef`/`AstNodeCCall` (currently used to access superclass methods/static variable, which would be nice. Do you have a suggestion on what would be the best way of doing this? I worry having  a `m_modp` inside an `AstCFunc`/`AstVar` that points to the module up the tree would complicate internals having to keep it in sync when movingthings around. Also `m_scopep` seems not available all the way to Emit.

EDIT: fixed typos
